---
title: "![](images/icons/icon_10.png){.title-icon} Case-control"
subtitle: "Nested case-control - sample controls for your cases"
date: last-modified
---

This page continues from [Build your study population](10_byg-din-kohorte.qmd). In a **nested case-control study**, your "exposed" group from the landing page is instead your **cases** (those who experienced the outcome). For each case you sample a number of **controls** - people without the outcome who were at risk exactly when the case had their outcome.

::: {.callout-warning}
**Still under development.** Structural guidance - adapt to your own project, and **confirm package names** (`Epi::ccwc`) on your project before use.
:::

::: {.callout-note}
**Cohort or case-control?**
Both designs can answer the same question; case-control is especially efficient when the outcome is rare, or when a covariate is expensive to extract for the whole cohort. See [Phase 1 - what type of study](01_studieforberedelse.qmd#studietype) for the design choice.
:::

<details>
<summary>**Case-control vs. nested case-control** - what is the difference?</summary>

- **Classic case-control:** you find cases (with the outcome) and select controls (without the outcome) from the population, often without a defined cohort with follow-up behind it. Exposure is typically ascertained retrospectively (e.g. interview) - vulnerable to recall and selection bias, and choosing controls is hard.
- **Nested case-control:** the case-control study sits *inside* a defined **cohort** (e.g. a registry cohort followed over time). Cases are those who develop the outcome during follow-up; controls are sampled from those who were at risk (outcome-free) exactly when the case had the outcome (risk-set / incidence-density sampling). Exposure comes from registry data collected *before* the outcome → less recall bias.

In register research at DST you almost always do the **nested** variant: the cohort already exists in the registers. Advantages: exposure is measured beforehand, and the odds ratio from incidence-density sampling estimates the rate/hazard ratio without a rare-outcome assumption. That is what this page shows.

</details>

---

## The principle: incidence-density sampling

Controls are selected by **risk-set / incidence-density sampling**: for each case, you draw controls who, on the case's index date, (1) have not yet had the outcome and (2) meet the inclusion criteria. A person can be a control for one case and *later* become a case themselves - that is correct in this design.

This is the **same machinery** as the risk-set matching on [Comparison cohort](10a_sammenligningskohorte.qmd): you identify cases (landing page, Step 1), exclude prevalent cases, build a pool from BEF and apply inclusion criteria **before** sampling. The only difference is that the "event" is now the *outcome* (case), not an exposure.

---

## Two routes to sampling

- **`heaven::riskSetMatch()`** - the same function as on [Comparison cohort](10a_sammenligningskohorte.qmd), with `event = "case"` (1 = case, 0 = potential control) and your matching variables in `terms`. Convenient if you already use it.
- **`Epi::ccwc()`** - a function aimed at **nested case-control** (risk-set sampling) from a cohort dataset with entry and exit times. Confirm the package and arguments on your project (`?Epi::ccwc`).

Typically match on sex, birth year and calendar time - and optionally SES/municipality as the case had them on their index date.

---

## Analysis (briefly)

Matched case-control data are analysed with **conditional logistic regression** (`survival::clogit()`), so the matching is respected. The analysis itself is outside this page - see the upcoming analysis section.

---

## See also

- [Build your study population](10_byg-din-kohorte.qmd) - identify your cases (Step 1)
- [Comparison cohort](10a_sammenligningskohorte.qmd) - the same risk-set machinery for a cohort design
- [Phase 1 - Study preparation](01_studieforberedelse.qmd) - cohort vs. case-control
- [Heide-Jørgensen et al. (2018), *Clinical Epidemiology* 10:1325-1337](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/30310326/) - sampling strategies and bias in risk-set sampling
